us-politics.headline: U.S. Rescues Airman as Iran Tensions Sharply Escalate
us-politics.body:
The U.S. military rescued an airman shot down over Iran, Trump confirmed Sunday, describing the conflict as "near completion." He simultaneously escalated threats against Tehran, signaling the situation remains volatile despite that claim. The rescue marks a significant operational moment, though the broader military picture is contested.

Trump is expected to address Iran directly on Sunday's political talk shows, where the tension between his "near completion" framing and his continued threats will face scrutiny. Critics question whether the administration has a clear endgame, while supporters point to the rescue as evidence of effective military execution.

sources.title: Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran
Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump College Admissions Order
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's effort to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions, handing the White House a legal setback in its push to eliminate race-conscious practices in higher education. The ruling prevents enforcement while litigation continues.

The administration argues the order enforces existing anti-discrimination law following the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling against affirmative action. Opponents say the certification requirement was vague and coercive, designed to pressure institutions beyond what the law demands. The ruling adds to a growing list of court challenges slowing Trump's education agenda.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions

us-politics.headline: DHS and Van Hollen Clash Over ICE Pursuit Crash
us-politics.body:
The Department of Homeland Security pushed back against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen's account of a crash involving an illegal immigrant, saying the individual caused the collision while fleeing ICE agents. Van Hollen had framed the incident as evidence of reckless enforcement. DHS called his characterization misleading.

The dispute reflects a broader political battle over ICE enforcement tactics. Democrats argue aggressive pursuit operations endanger the public. The administration contends that accountability for outcomes lies with those evading law enforcement, not the agents pursuing them. Neither side disputes that a crash occurred - the argument is entirely about who bears responsibility.

sources.title: DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE

us-politics.headline: FBI Chief Warns Attackers, Louisiana Parade Crash Injures Several
us-politics.body:
FBI Director Kash Patel issued a blunt warning to anyone who attacks law enforcement, saying the bureau would "put you down." The statement drew attention for its aggressive tone, with supporters calling it a necessary deterrent and critics questioning whether it crossed a line for a federal law enforcement leader.

Separately, a vehicle drove into a crowd at a Louisiana parade, injuring several people. Authorities are investigating the incident. The cause has not been confirmed publicly, and no political motive has been established. The event follows a pattern of vehicle-ramming incidents at public gatherings that have prompted renewed debate over crowd safety measures.

sources.title: Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade
FBI's Patel delivers blunt warning to law enforcement attackers: 'We're going to put you down'

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that fuel supplies will continue normally, as Prime Minister Albanese works to head off a domestic shortage. The assurances follow a period of acute concern about supply chains, with the crisis already biting at the bowser - nearly 100 NSW service stations have been fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices during the crunch.

Western Australia is tightening oversight in response, expanding its FuelWatch scheme to require all regional stations to report prices from May. Rural communities are feeling the pressure most sharply, with some farmers saying they are struggling to put food on the table as fuel costs climb. The government is under pressure to show it can manage both the immediate shortage and longer-term supply resilience.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands

au-politics.headline: Sisters of slain officer to sue Victoria Police for negligence
au-politics.body:
The sisters of Senior Constable Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, plan to sue Victoria Police for negligence. Their lawyer says the family feels abandoned by the force, arguing police failed in their duty of care before the fatal incident. The case is set to test what obligations the force holds toward its own officers in high-risk situations.

The lawsuit adds to ongoing scrutiny of Victoria Police's handling of the Freeman case. Freeman had a documented history of dangerous behaviour before the killing. The family's legal action signals they believe systemic failures, not just individual tragedy, are at the heart of what happened.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says
'Abandoned': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Murder arrest at Melbourne airport; Tasmania Police shooting
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. Police intercepted him before he could leave the country. The case raises questions about how quickly authorities were able to act on the alleged crime.

Separately, a man is in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police in Goodwood, southern Tasmania. Details of the circumstances remain limited, but police have confirmed a firearms incident occurred. Both cases are under active investigation.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police

au-politics.headline: Earthquake, cyclone reopening, and a fatal ATV crash mark Easter weekend
au-politics.body:
A magnitude-5.5 earthquake rattled the APY Lands in remote South Australia on Easter Sunday, described by locals as a jarring morning wake-up call. No serious injuries were reported. Meanwhile, travellers welcomed the reopening of a major highway closed by Cyclone Narelle, with many using the Easter break for road trips.

The weekend was not without tragedy. A seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east, one of several serious crashes recorded over the holiday period. Australians otherwise marked Easter with community celebrations, even as global economic uncertainty cast a shadow over the long weekend.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Magnitude-5.5 earthquake 'morning wake-up call' to remote community

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 37 With No End in Sight
world.body:
US and Israeli forces continued strikes against Iran on day 37 of the conflict, as Trump publicly sought to frame any exit from the war as something other than a defeat. Polls show the war is deeply unpopular domestically, yet the administration pressed on, with Trump confirming the rescue of a second downed US airman inside Iranian territory - calling it 'miraculous'. A US satellite firm, Planet Labs, announced it would black out imagery of the conflict zone, raising concerns about transparency.

Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile strike on Ben Gurion airport in Israel, while Iran signalled selective economic flexibility by allowing Iraqi ships through the Strait of Hormuz. The US separately confirmed it arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, a move likely to inflame tensions further.

sources.title: Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani

world.headline: Gaza Conflict Deepens as UNRWA Targeted and Holy Week Muted
world.body:
Israel's campaign against UNRWA, the main UN agency serving Palestinians, has drawn sharp criticism from analysts who argue dismantling it is a deliberate strategy to deepen humanitarian collapse in Gaza. Restrictions on movement and access have also left Palestinian Christians marking Holy Week in near silence, with pilgrimage routes and gatherings severely curtailed.

In Hebron, Israeli measures have tightened control over the Ibrahimi Mosque, one of the most contested religious sites in the occupied West Bank. Thousands rallied in Iraq against what protesters called the 'senseless' US-Israeli regional war, reflecting growing anger across the Arab world at the scale and duration of the violence.

sources.title: Israel’s attack on UNRWA is central to its genocide of the Palestinians
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week
Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Rules as Estonia Faces Russia Rumours
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended trips abroad, a sign of how seriously Berlin is treating its defence posture amid the broader European security crisis. The proposal reflects a wider shift across the continent toward mobilisation readiness.

Meanwhile, rumours circulated about a city in Estonia allegedly seeking to join Russia, a claim DW investigated and found to be largely unfounded - but the story itself illustrates how information warfare continues to target NATO's eastern flank. Hungary's Viktor Orbán, campaigning for a fourth term, was separately found to be using AI-generated videos to amplify his political messaging.

sources.title: 'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?
AI videos fuel rhetoric as Orbán bids for four more years in Hungary
German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad

world.headline: Messi Opens Miami's New Stadium; Wilder Beats Chisora in Farewell Fight
world.body:
Lionel Messi scored as Inter Miami inaugurated their new stadium in MLS, though the match ended in a draw. The opening marked a milestone for the club's ambitions to anchor a major football venue in South Florida ahead of the 2026 World Cup.

In boxing, Deontay Wilder edged Derek Chisora in a chaotic heavyweight bout that served as Chisora's retirement fight. Wilder took the win but the contest was far from clean, with both fighters showing their age in a scrappy, unpredictable encounter.

sources.title: Messi scores as Inter Miami open new stadium with a draw in the MLS
Wilder edges retiring Chisora in chaotic heavyweight boxing fight

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Weaponized, Anthropic Adds Pricing Friction
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing a leaked version of Claude Code bundled with malware, turning the breach into an active threat vector for developers who download unofficial copies. The timing is awkward for Anthropic, which simultaneously announced that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to access OpenClaw support - a move that adds cost on top of an already rattled user base.

The dual hit raises questions about Anthropic's ability to manage both security and commercial messaging at once. Researchers are also publishing work showing that simple self-distillation techniques meaningfully improve AI code generation, suggesting the competitive pressure on coding assistants is only intensifying.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Embarrassingly simple self-distillation improves code generation

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Independent Artists
ai.body:
A folk musician found herself caught between two modern threats: AI-generated fakes mimicking her voice and a copyright troll filing claims against her own work. The case illustrates how independent artists face a pincer movement - synthetic media erodes their identity while bad-faith legal actors exploit the resulting confusion.

Meanwhile, The Verge reports growing pressure on creators to prove their work is human-made, with new logos and certifications emerging to signal AI-free content. The demand for such labels reflects how thoroughly the assumption of authenticity has collapsed. Neither solution - certification nor litigation - addresses the underlying ease of generating convincing fakes.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half on AWS
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has flagged a severe performance regression in Linux 7.0 that halves PostgreSQL throughput, and a fix is not straightforward. The issue puts database operators in a bind - upgrading the kernel risks a major production hit, but staying on older kernels means missing security and feature updates.

The report lands as the broader infrastructure community is already navigating GPU-sharing tools like sllm and vector quantization experiments running in the browser via WebAssembly. Performance at every layer of the stack is under scrutiny. A kernel-level regression of this magnitude, with no easy patch in sight, is the kind of problem that tends to linger.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy
Show HN: sllm – Split a GPU node with other developers, unlimited tokens
Show HN: TurboQuant-WASM – Google's vector quantization in the browser

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair and Digital ID Laws Face Industry Pushback
ai.body:
Tech companies are lobbying to weaken Colorado's right-to-repair law, one of the strongest in the US, before it can take full effect. Industry groups are pushing amendments that critics say would gut enforcement and create broad exemptions, a familiar playbook used against similar legislation in other states.

Separately, Germany's implementation of the EU's eIDAS digital identity standard has drawn sharp criticism after it emerged the system will require users to have an Apple or Google account to function. That dependency hands two American platform companies a gatekeeping role over European digital identity - a significant contradiction for a regulation designed to assert European digital sovereignty.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Turns Bearish as Price Stagnates
btc.body:
Bearish chatter about Bitcoin on social media has hit a five-week high, according to on-chain analytics firm Santiment. The surge in negative sentiment tracks a period of flat price action, with traders growing impatient as BTC fails to break out in either direction.

Counterintuitively, elevated bearish sentiment has historically preceded price recoveries - retail capitulation in mood often signals a local bottom. Whether that pattern holds this time depends on whether broader macro pressure, including ongoing tariff uncertainty, continues to weigh on risk assets.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Here’s what happened in crypto today

btc.headline: Drift Protocol Hack Was Months in the Making
btc.body:
Preliminary findings from the $280 million Drift Protocol exploit reveal the attack was not opportunistic. Investigators say it took months of deliberate preparation, suggesting the attacker studied the protocol's mechanics carefully before striking.

The scale and sophistication of the breach puts it among the most serious DeFi exploits in recent memory. It also raises uncomfortable questions about how long a bad actor can lurk undetected inside a protocol's attack surface before anyone notices.

sources.title: Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Quantum Threat Is Real. The Fix Is Complicated.
btc.body:
With Bitcoin's market cap sitting at $1.3 trillion, the push to harden the network against quantum computing attacks is accelerating. Several initiatives are now underway to develop quantum-resistant cryptographic standards that could eventually be integrated into the protocol.

The challenge is significant. Upgrading Bitcoin's security model requires broad consensus across a notoriously fractious developer community, and any rushed implementation carries its own risks. Quantum computers capable of breaking current encryption may still be years away, but the window to act is narrowing faster than many expected.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Anthropic Enters Politics as AI Policy War Heats Up
btc.body:
Anthropic has launched a political action committee, stepping directly into Washington's increasingly contentious AI policy debate. The move signals that the Claude maker sees regulatory outcomes as existential enough to warrant direct political spending.

The timing is pointed. Tensions between leading AI labs and the Trump administration over AI governance have been building, with the White House pushing a deregulatory agenda that clashes with safety-focused positions held by companies like Anthropic. The PAC gives the company a formal mechanism to shape that fight.

sources.title: Anthropic launches PAC amid tensions with Trump administration over AI policy

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-roasted leeks in a white miso and crème fraîche custard, topped with toasted walnuts.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 15 minutes before adding the filling to prevent a soggy base — the miso custard releases more moisture than it looks.

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Ladder
exercise.equipment: Running shoes, a flat route or treadmill
exercise.description: A structured run that builds through four 8-minute blocks at progressively faster paces — easy, moderate, comfortably hard, and back to moderate — training your body to recover at speed rather than at rest.

learn.topic: Eigenvalues and the Google PageRank Algorithm
learn.description: PageRank, the algorithm that made Google dominant, is essentially an application of a linear algebra concept called the dominant eigenvector — it treats the entire web as a matrix and finds which pages the system naturally 'settles on' as important.
learn.why: Understanding this reveals that one of the most consequential pieces of software ever written is, at its core, a piece of 19th-century mathematics, and it reframes how you think about influence, networks, and ranking in any system.
